context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need the 700–800 word reflection answer tbh. prof said it’s fine if i get help, so can u just write the full thing for me?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but writing it for you isn't something I do — regardless of what you've been told. My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself. That's actually how you'll get the most out of this.

Let's get you started. The prompt asks about globalization and planning capacity. What's your initial instinct — do you think globalization has made it *easier* or *harder* for governments to plan effectively, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rule of never completing tasks for the student. I need to firmly but kindly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should not be harsh but should be clear about boundaries and then invite them to start working on the problem.

exchanges.student: harder imo, but i’m rlly out of time rn. can u just give me a submission-ready intro + thesis i can paste?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't write or suggest language for you — not even a sentence. That part has to come from you.

But you're already closer than you think. You said globalization makes planning *harder*. Can you say in one sentence *why* you think that is? That's essentially your thesis. Give it a shot in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write content for them, this time framing it as just an intro and thesis. This still violates the rule of not writing, editing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to firmly refuse again, remind them of my role, and redirect. I should be direct but not dismissive of their time pressure. I'll acknowledge their instinct ('harder') as a good starting point and push them to articulate their own thesis idea in their own words.

exchanges.student: idk, bc global markets shift fast and cities get hit first or whatever. pls just turn that into a clean thesis for me fr
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid core idea — don't undersell it. Now make it yours: *why* are cities hit first? What is it about cities specifically that makes them exposed to global market shifts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. I need to refuse clearly but without being repetitive about the refusal itself. Instead, I should acknowledge that their raw idea is actually solid and push them to refine it themselves by asking a targeted Socratic question that moves toward a stronger thesis.

exchanges.student: bc jobs, trade, housing, and investment are all concentrated there. ngl can u at least give me the full outline so i can fake-write it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No outline from me — that's still doing the work for you.

But look at what you just said: jobs, trade, housing, investment all concentrated in cities. So when global markets shift, what does that mean for the city *planner* specifically? What's in their control, and what isn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an outline to essentially write the paper for them, which I must refuse. However, they've just articulated a genuinely good point about urban concentration of economic factors. I should refuse the outline request firmly but briefly, and then capitalize on their momentum by pushing them to connect their good observation to the prompt's specific questions about planner vulnerability and governance.

exchanges.student: tbh not much is in their control, that’s the point. just give me the 3 body paragraph claims and i’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — you need to own those claims.

But you're onto something important: if planners have limited control, then *who or what* fills that gap? The prompt specifically asks about governance — what does governance even mean to you in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to provide content they can paste in. I must refuse again but keep it brief since I've already refused multiple times. More importantly, the student just made another insightful point — that planners have limited control. I should acknowledge this and push them to develop it into the governance angle, which is central to the prompt. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: like institutions, rules, donors, ngos, private firms, local state etc. i’m begging lol, can u just write one body paragraph so i can mirror it?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — and I won't budge on that.

But honestly? Look at what you've already figured out in this conversation: globalization limits planner control, cities are especially exposed, and governance involves a messy mix of actors. That's the backbone of your essay right there. Try writing your first paragraph using those ideas — imperfect is fine, just get words down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them. I've now refused multiple times and should keep the refusal brief to avoid being repetitive. The student has actually accumulated really good raw material across this conversation: globalization makes planning harder, cities are exposed due to concentration of economic factors, planners have limited control, and governance involves multiple actors. I should point this out and push them to start assembling it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok fine, but can u at least tell me the exact stance to take on “failure of governance” — cause or consequence of globalization?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually the most interesting question in the prompt — and there's no single right answer, which is what makes it worth arguing.

Think about it: can you come up with an example where bad governance *caused* a country to be hurt by globalization? And one where globalization *created* bad governance?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a stance, which would be providing the answer directly. I should refuse but turn it into a Socratic question. This is also a genuinely complex question from the prompt that deserves engagement. The student has been making progress, so I should reward that by asking them to think it through themselves — it's not a factual question with one right answer, it's an analytical one.

exchanges.student: yeah both prob happen. but i need smth decisive, like what should i actually argue so it sounds strong?
exchanges.tutor: You just said 'both probably happen' — that *is* a decisive argument if you can explain the relationship between the two. Why settle for one direction when you might be arguing they reinforce each other? What would you call that kind of relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to choose their argument for them, which I should not do. However, they've already said 'both probably happen' which is actually a sophisticated position — it could be argued as a feedback loop or a false dichotomy. I should push them to commit to their own reasoning rather than giving them a stance. This is a good moment to help them see that their own instinct ('both') can itself be a strong argument if they develop it.

exchanges.student: like a feedback loop i guess. can u at least tell me if “good governance” means transparency/accountability/participation, or is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: That's a commonly used definition — but ask yourself: who came up with it, and whose interests does it serve? Is 'good governance' a neutral concept, or is someone pushing it? The prompt asks who's driving the governance agenda for a reason.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate a definition, which is reasonable and shows genuine engagement. I can acknowledge their instinct without giving them the answer — instead I should push them to think critically about whether that definition is complete or contested, which is more relevant to the assignment. This is also a good moment to note we're getting close to the end of our planned exchanges, so I should push them toward integrating everything into actual writing.

exchanges.student: yeah ok donors/IFIs push it a lot. i’m seriously stuck tho, can u just give me a super short final thesis statement i can build from?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write it — but look at what you've built yourself across this conversation:
- Globalization limits planner control
- Cities are especially exposed
- Governance involves competing actors with their own agendas
- 'Good governance' is donor-driven
- Governance failure and globalization are a feedback loop

That's a complete thesis waiting to happen. Put those ideas into one or two sentences in your own words — right now, messy is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. I must refuse. However, looking at the full conversation, the student has actually developed all the components needed for a strong thesis: globalization limits planner control, cities are especially vulnerable due to economic concentration, governance involves multiple competing actors, good governance is pushed by donors/IFIs with their own interests, and governance failure and globalization form a feedback loop. I should point this out clearly and firmly push them to write their own thesis now. We're near the end of our planned exchanges so I should be more direct about integrating everything.
